summary: Jeff Atwood draws a parallel between MMORPGs like World of Warcraft and corporate programming culture, arguing that both wrongly reward time investment over actual skill. He uses the satirical game ProgressQuest to illustrate how absurd pure time-based progression is, then cites Weinberg's classic example of a programmer who spent 8 weeks creating a mess that another developer rewrote in one week with better results. The post warns against the 'EverQuest Syndrome' in software development, where long hours are mistaken for productivity and even rewarded by management. Atwood concludes that becoming a better programmer requires building genuine skills, not grinding through all-nighters.
key_points: Time invested in programming has little correlation with actual value produced - a skilled developer can create 10x more value than an average one working longer hours
The 'EverQuest Syndrome' describes the fallacy of equating long hours with good work, a trap both gamers and programmers fall into
Management often rewards visible effort (long hours) rather than actual results, leading to morale problems when the truth emerges
ProgressQuest satirizes pure time-based progression systems by removing all gameplay and focusing only on leveling up
Building a skill portfolio is the path to improvement, not grinding through marathon coding sessions
money_quotes: The very idea that time > skill is alien.
Just because you spend a lot of time doing something doesn't mean you're doing it well.
If you want to level up in your IDE, you can't do it by grinding through all-nighters. You have to build your skill portfolio.
Clearly, the first programmer had been rewarded for making a mountain out of a molehill.
The rewriting and debugging took exactly one week, working normal hours. Even considering that writing a program for the second time is far easier, the difference is significant.
The discovery of this misapplication of management largesse led to a severe drop in morale.
key_insight: Programming skill development comes from deliberate improvement and building expertise, not from logging more hours at the keyboard.
